event: Early modern humans, ancestors of later populations, reach the island of Timor as part of the broader migration through the islands of Wallacea toward Australia and New Guinea.
Archaeological sites such as Asitau Kuru (Jerimalai) cave show occupation, with evidence of sophisticated deep-sea fishing for tuna and the world's earliest known fish hooks.
Pleistocene foragers in eastern Timor exploit marine and terrestrial resources, leaving shell middens and stone tools documenting continuous coastal habitation.
Austronesian-speaking seafarers expand into Timor, introducing new languages, pottery, domesticated animals, and agricultural practices alongside the existing Papuan-related populations.
A mix of Austronesian and Papuan (Trans-New Guinea) language groups becomes established, forming the linguistic mosaic that includes Tetum, Mambai, Makasae and other tongues.
Timor becomes integrated into regional maritime trade networks, prized especially for its abundant sandalwood, which is exchanged with Chinese, Javanese and Malay merchants.
The Chinese work Zhu Fan Zhi by Zhao Rukuo records Timor as a source of fragrant sandalwood, documenting its place in Asian commerce.
Portuguese traders and missionaries begin visiting Timor, drawn by its sandalwood, marking the start of sustained European contact.
Dominican friars establish a settlement and mission at Lifau in the enclave region (modern Oecusse), spreading Catholicism and Portuguese influence.
The Dutch East India Company establishes a presence on western Timor, beginning the long contest between Dutch and Portuguese spheres on the island.
Portugal appoints its first governor to Timor, formalizing colonial administration over the eastern part of the island.
After conflict with the Topasses (Eurasian Catholic community), the Portuguese relocate their colonial capital from Lifau eastward to Dili.
The Treaty of Lisbon between Portugal and the Netherlands partitions Timor, dividing the island into Portuguese (east) and Dutch (west) zones.
A further Hague convention refines the colonial boundary, confirming the Oecusse enclave and settling the Portuguese-Dutch frontier on Timor.
Portugal suppresses the major Manufahi (Boaventura) rebellion, the largest indigenous uprising against Portuguese rule, with heavy Timorese casualties.
Japanese forces invade and occupy Portuguese Timor during World War II; Australian and Dutch commandos wage a guerrilla campaign assisted by Timorese, leading to tens of thousands of local deaths.
Japan surrenders at the end of World War II, and Portuguese colonial administration is restored over East Timor.
Portugal's Carnation Revolution ushers in decolonization, prompting the formation of Timorese political parties including Fretilin, UDT and Apodeti.
After a brief civil war and a Fretilin victory, the Democratic Republic of East Timor declares independence on 28 November, but Indonesia invades and occupies the territory days later on 7 December.
Indonesia formally annexes East Timor as its 27th province (Timor Timur), an act never recognized by the United Nations.
Xanana Gusmao becomes leader of the armed resistance (Falintil), reorganizing the guerrilla and clandestine struggle against Indonesian rule.
Indonesian troops massacre peaceful pro-independence demonstrators at Santa Cruz cemetery in Dili, an atrocity filmed and broadcast worldwide that galvanizes international attention.
Resistance leader Xanana Gusmao is captured by Indonesian forces and later imprisoned, becoming a symbol of the independence movement.
Bishop Carlos Filipe Ximenes Belo and activist Jose Ramos-Horta are jointly awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for their work toward a just and peaceful resolution to the conflict.

In a UN-supervised referendum, nearly 79 percent of East Timorese vote for independence; pro-Indonesian militias unleash a campaign of violence, prompting deployment of the Australian-led INTERFET force and UN administration (UNTAET).
East Timor (Timor-Leste) formally restores its independence on 20 May, with Xanana Gusmao as first president, and is admitted to the United Nations as its 191st member on 27 September.
A crisis erupts after the dismissal of hundreds of soldiers, sparking factional fighting and unrest in Dili and the return of international peacekeepers.
Jose Ramos-Horta is elected president and Xanana Gusmao becomes prime minister, marking a peaceful democratic transfer of leadership.
President Ramos-Horta is shot and seriously wounded in an assassination attempt by rebel soldiers, but survives and recovers.
The United Nations peacekeeping mission (UNMIT) departs as Timor-Leste assumes full responsibility for its own security following peaceful elections.
Timor-Leste and Australia sign a landmark maritime boundary treaty resolving disputes over the resource-rich Timor Sea and the Greater Sunrise gas fields.
Timor-Leste records its first cases of COVID-19 and imposes a state of emergency, while remaining one of the least-affected nations early in the pandemic.
Severe flooding from Tropical Cyclone Seroja devastates Dili and surrounding areas, killing dozens and displacing thousands, underscoring vulnerability to intensifying climate-driven extreme weather.
Jose Ramos-Horta is elected president for a second term as the young nation marks twenty years of restored independence.
Xanana Gusmao returns as prime minister after his CNRT party wins parliamentary elections, while Timor-Leste advances its bid to join ASEAN.
Timor-Leste is formally admitted as the eleventh member of ASEAN, marking a major milestone in its regional integration.
